**Greater India**, also known as the **Indian cultural sphere**, or the **Indic world**, is an area composed of several countries and regions in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") that were historically influenced by [Indian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_India "Culture of India"), which itself formed from the various distinct indigenous cultures of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is an umbrella term encompassing the Indian subcontinent and surrounding countries, which are culturally linked through a diverse cultural cline. These countries have been transformed to varying degrees by the adoption and incorporation of [cultural](/wiki/Culture "Culture") and institutional elements from each other.

From around 500 BCE, Asia's expanding land and [maritime trade](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade "Indian Ocean trade") had resulted in prolonged [socio-economic](/wiki/Socioeconomics "Socioeconomics") and cultural stimulation and diffusion of [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Hindu](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") beliefs into the region's cosmology, in particular in Southeast Asia and the Far-East.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In Central Asia, the transmission of ideas was predominantly of a religious nature and short-lived, often co-existing with native philosophies such as Zoroastrianism and being quickly supplanted by the rise of Islam. In contrast, the spread of native Indian culture to East Asia was more multifaced and involved wide-ranging cultural exchange beyond religion.

By the early centuries of the [common era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"), most of the principalities of Southeast Asia had effectively absorbed defining aspects of Indian culture, religion, and administration. The notion of divine god-kingship was introduced by the concept of [Harihara](/wiki/Harihara "Harihara"), and Sanskrit and other Indian [epigraphic](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") systems were declared [official](/wiki/Official_script "Official script"), like those of the south Indian [Pallava dynasty](/wiki/Pallava_dynasty "Pallava dynasty") and [Chalukya dynasty](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty").[[6]](#cite_note-Lavy-2003-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Stark-1999-7) These [Indianized](/wiki/Indianization_of_Southeast_Asia "Indianization of Southeast Asia") kingdoms, a term coined by [George Cœdès](/wiki/George_C%C5%93d%C3%A8s "George Cœdès") in his work *Histoire ancienne des états hindouisés d'Extrême-Orient*,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoedès196814–-8) were characterized by resilience, political integrity, and administrative stability.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

To the north, Indian religious ideas were assimilated into the cosmology of Himalayan peoples, most profoundly in Tibet and Bhutan, and merged with indigenous traditions. [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") [monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") extended into [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"), and other parts of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), and Buddhist texts and ideas were accepted in China and Japan in the east.[[10]](#cite_note-10) To the west, Indian culture converged with [Greater Persia](/wiki/Greater_Persia "Greater Persia") via the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") and the [Pamir Mountains](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Evolution of the concept

### Geographical designation

Further information: [Indies](/wiki/Indies "Indies") and [Geography (Ptolemy)](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/1864_Mitchell_Map_of_India%2C_Tibet%2C_China_and_Southeast_Asia_-_Geographicus_-_India-mitchell-1864.jpg/330px-1864_Mitchell_Map_of_India%2C_Tibet%2C_China_and_Southeast_Asia_-_Geographicus_-_India-mitchell-1864.jpg)](/wiki/File:1864_Mitchell_Map_of_India,_Tibet,_China_and_Southeast_Asia_-_Geographicus_-_India-mitchell-1864.jpg)

[Hindoostan](/wiki/Hindoostan "Hindoostan") and [Farther India](/wiki/Farther_India "Farther India") in a 1864 map by [Samuel Augustus Mitchell](/wiki/Samuel_Augustus_Mitchell "Samuel Augustus Mitchell")

The concept of the *Three Indias* was in common circulation in pre-industrial Europe. *Greater India* was the [southern part of South Asia](/wiki/Southern_South_Asia "Southern South Asia"), *Lesser India* was the [northern part of South Asia](/wiki/Northern_South_Asia "Northern South Asia"), and *Middle India* was the [region near the Middle East](/wiki/Northwestern_South_Asia "Northwestern South Asia").[[12]](#cite_note-Phillips-1998-12) The Portuguese form ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *India Maior*[[12]](#cite_note-Phillips-1998-12)[[13]](#cite_note-azurara-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)) was used at least since the mid-15th century.[[13]](#cite_note-azurara-13) The term, which seems to have been used with variable precision,[[16]](#cite_note-16) sometimes meant only the Indian subcontinent;[[17]](#cite_note-17) Europeans used a variety of terms related to South Asia to designate the South Asian peninsula, including *High India*, *Greater India*, *Exterior India* and *India aquosa*.[[18]](#cite_note-Lewis-1997-18)

However, in some accounts of European nautical voyages, Greater India (or *India Major*) extended from the [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast") (present-day [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala")) to *India extra Gangem*[[19]](#cite_note-19) (lit. "India, beyond the Ganges," but usually the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies"), i.e. present-day [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago")) and *India Minor*, from Malabar to [Sind](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh").[[20]](#cite_note-20) *[Farther India](/wiki/Farther_India "Farther India")* was sometimes used to cover all of modern Southeast Asia.[[18]](#cite_note-Lewis-1997-18) Until the fourteenth century, India could also mean areas along the Red Sea, including [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), [South Arabia](/wiki/South_Arabia "South Arabia"), and [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") (e.g., Diodorus of Sicily of the first century BC says that "the Nile rises in India" and Marco Polo of the fourteenth century says that "Lesser India ... contains ... Abash [Abyssinia]").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In late 19th-century geography, *Greater India* referred to a region that included: "(a) [Himalaya](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas"), (b) [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab"), (c) [Hindustan](/wiki/Hindustan "Hindustan"), (d) [Burma](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), (e) [Indo-China](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia"), (f) [Sunda Islands](/wiki/Sunda_Islands "Sunda Islands"), (g) [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), (h) [Celebes](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi"), and (i) [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines")."[[22]](#cite_note-22) German atlases distinguished *Vorder-Indien* (Anterior India) as the South Asian peninsula and *Hinter-Indien* as Southeast Asia.[[18]](#cite_note-Lewis-1997-18)

### Geological connotation

*Greater India*, or *Greater India Basin* also signifies "the [Indian Plate](/wiki/Indian_Plate "Indian Plate") plus a postulated northern extension", the product of the *Indian–Asia collision*.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Although its usage in geology pre-dates [Plate tectonic](/wiki/Plate_tectonic "Plate tectonic") theory,[[24]](#cite_note-24) the term has seen increased usage since the 1970s. It is unknown when and where the India–Asia (Indian and [Eurasian Plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate")) convergence occurred, at or before 52 million years ago. The plates have converged up to 3,600 km (2,200 mi) ± 35 km (22 mi). The upper crustal shortening is documented from geological record of Asia and the Himalaya as up to approximately 2,350 km (1,460 mi) less.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Cultural sphere

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/006_Bujang_Valley_Candi.jpg/250px-006_Bujang_Valley_Candi.jpg)](/wiki/File:006_Bujang_Valley_Candi.jpg)

Candi Bukit Batu Pahat of [Bujang Valley](/wiki/Bujang_Valley "Bujang Valley"). A [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu")-[Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") kingdom ruled ancient [Kedah](/wiki/Kedah "Kedah") possibly as early as 110 CE, the earliest evidence of strong Indian influence which was once prevalent among the [Kedahan Malays](/wiki/Kedahan_Malay "Kedahan Malay").

The use of *Greater India* to refer to an Indian cultural sphere was popularised by a network of Bengali scholars in the 1920s who were all members of the Calcutta-based Greater India Society. The movement's early leaders included the historian [R. C. Majumdar](/wiki/R._C._Majumdar "R. C. Majumdar") (1888–1980); the philologists [Suniti Kumar Chatterji](/wiki/Suniti_Kumar_Chatterji "Suniti Kumar Chatterji") (1890–1977) and [P. C. Bagchi](/wiki/Prabodh_Chandra_Bagchi "Prabodh Chandra Bagchi") (1898–1956), and the historians [Phanindranath Bose](/w/index.php?title=Phanindranath_Bose&action=edit&redlink=1 "Phanindranath Bose (page does not exist)") and [Kalidas Nag](/wiki/Kalidas_Nag "Kalidas Nag") (1891–1966).[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Some of their formulations were inspired by concurrent excavations in [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor") by French archaeologists and by the writings of French [Indologist](/wiki/Indology "Indology") [Sylvain Lévi](/wiki/Sylvain_L%C3%A9vi "Sylvain Lévi"). The scholars of the society postulated a benevolent ancient Indian cultural colonisation of Southeast Asia, in stark contrast – in their view – to the Western colonialism of the early 20th century.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

By some accounts Greater India consists of "lands including Burma, [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), Cambodia, [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali"), and the former [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa") and [Funan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Funan "Kingdom of Funan") polities of present-day [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"),"[[31]](#cite_note-bayley2004-p713-31) in which Indian and Hindu culture left an "imprint in the form of monuments, inscriptions and other traces of the historic "[Indianizing](/wiki/Sanskritization "Sanskritization")" process."[[31]](#cite_note-bayley2004-p713-31) By some other accounts, many Pacific societies and "most of the Buddhist world including [Ceylon](/wiki/Ceylon "Ceylon"), Tibet, Central Asia, and even Japan were held to fall within this web of Indianizing *culture colonies*"[[31]](#cite_note-bayley2004-p713-31) This particular usage – implying cultural "sphere of influence" of India – was promoted by the [Greater India Society](/w/index.php?title=Greater_India_Society&action=edit&redlink=1 "Greater India Society (page does not exist)"), formed by a group of [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_people "Bengali people") [men of letters](/wiki/Man_of_letters "Man of letters"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) and is not found before the 1920s. The term *Greater India* was used in historical writing in India into the 1970s.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Indianization

Main article: [Hindu kingship](/wiki/Hindu_kingship "Hindu kingship")

The concept of "Indianized kingdoms" and "Indianization", coined by [George Coedès](/wiki/George_Coed%C3%A8s "George Coedès"), originally describes Southeast Asian [principalities](/wiki/Principality "Principality") that flourished from the early common era as a result of centuries of socio-economic interaction having incorporated central aspects of Indian institutions, religion, statecraft, administration, culture, epigraphy, literature and architecture.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Expansionist and political concept

See also: [Akhand Bharat](/wiki/Akhand_Bharat "Akhand Bharat")

The term *Greater India* and the notion of an explicit Hindu expansion of ancient Southeast Asia have been linked to both [Indian nationalism](/wiki/Indian_nationalism "Indian nationalism")[[36]](#cite_note-36) and [Hindu nationalism](/wiki/Hindu_nationalism "Hindu nationalism").[[37]](#cite_note-37) The English term was popularised in the late 19th and the 20th century as a view of an expansionist India within the context of East Asia.[[38]](#cite_note-Zabarskaitė-2022-38) However, many Indian nationalists, like [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru") and [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore"), although receptive to "an idealisation of India as a benign and uncoercive world civiliser and font of global enlightenment,"[[39]](#cite_note-39) stayed away from explicit "Greater India" formulations.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In addition, some scholars have seen the Hindu/Buddhist acculturation in ancient Southeast Asia as "a single cultural process in which Southeast Asia was the matrix and South Asia the mediatrix."[[41]](#cite_note-41) In the field of art history, especially in American writings, the term survived due to the influence of art theorist [Ananda Coomaraswamy](/wiki/Ananda_Coomaraswamy "Ananda Coomaraswamy"). Coomaraswamy's view of pan-Indian art history was influenced by the "Calcutta cultural nationalists."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Its modern meanings often invoke images of soft power.[[38]](#cite_note-Zabarskaitė-2022-38) The region is considered in Indian political circles as part of India's extended neighbourhood, and modern integration was propelled through a multifaceted acceleration of economic and strategic interaction under the "[Look East](/wiki/Look_East_policy_(India) "Look East policy (India)")" policy, and more recently has involved deepening military ties as well.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Sri Lanka also continues to have strong political links with Southeast Asia, asked by [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") to be a founding member, and has recently been increasing integration with Southeast Asia through its own "Look East" policy; politicians view the relationship between Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia as second only to South Asia.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Indian cultural influence

### Cultural expansion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Atashgah_Fire_Temple.jpg/250px-Atashgah_Fire_Temple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atashgah_Fire_Temple.jpg)

[Atashgah of Baku](/wiki/Atashgah_of_Baku "Atashgah of Baku"), a [fire temple](/wiki/Fire_temple "Fire temple") in Azerbaijan used by both Hindus[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) and Persian Zoroastrians

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Sculpture_and_mural_from_cave_254._Pillar_and_north_wall._Northern_Wei._Mogao.jpg/250px-Sculpture_and_mural_from_cave_254._Pillar_and_north_wall._Northern_Wei._Mogao.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sculpture_and_mural_from_cave_254._Pillar_and_north_wall._Northern_Wei._Mogao.jpg)

Hindu-Buddhist icongraphy from [Mogao Caves](/wiki/Mogao_Caves "Mogao Caves") in the [Gobi Desert](/wiki/Gobi_Desert "Gobi Desert")

Culture spread via the trade routes that linked India with southern [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"), central and southern [Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam"), the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") and [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") to [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), lower [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") and [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa"). The [Pali](/wiki/P%C4%81li "Pāli") and Sanskrit languages and the Indian script, together with [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") and [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Brahmanism](/wiki/Vedic_Brahmanism "Vedic Brahmanism") and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), were transmitted from direct contact as well as through sacred texts and Indian literature. Southeast Asia had developed some prosperous and very powerful colonial empires that contributed to Hindu-Buddhist artistic creations and architectural developments. Art and architectural creations that rivaled those built in India, especially in its sheer size, design and aesthetic achievements. The notable examples are Borobudur in Java and Angkor monuments in Cambodia. The Srivijaya Empire to the south and the [Khmer Empire](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire") to the north competed for influence in the region.

A defining characteristic of the cultural link between Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent was the adoption of ancient Indian [Vedic](/wiki/Vedic_period "Vedic period")/Hindu and Buddhist culture and philosophy into [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Malaya](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") and Cambodia. Indian scripts are found in Southeast Asian islands ranging from Sumatra, Java, Bali, South Sulawesi and the [Philippines](/wiki/Baybayin "Baybayin").[[49]](#cite_note-49) The [Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana") and the [Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata") have had a large impact on South Asia and Southeast Asia. One of the most tangible evidence of dharmic Hindu traditions is the widespread use of the *[Añjali Mudrā](/wiki/A%C3%B1jali_Mudr%C4%81 "Añjali Mudrā")* gesture of greeting and respect. It is seen in the [Indian](/wiki/Culture_of_India "Culture of India") *[namasté](/wiki/Namast%C3%A9 "Namasté")* and similar gestures known throughout Southeast Asia; its cognates include the [Cambodian](/wiki/Culture_of_Cambodia "Culture of Cambodia") *[sampeah](/wiki/Sampeah "Sampeah")*, the [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_culture "Indonesian culture") *[sembah](/wiki/Sembah "Sembah")*, the [Japanese](/wiki/Culture_of_Japan "Culture of Japan") *[gassho](/wiki/Buddhist_terms_and_concepts#G "Buddhist terms and concepts")* and [Thai](/wiki/Culture_of_Thailand "Culture of Thailand") [*wai*](/wiki/Thai_greeting "Thai greeting").

Beyond the [Himalaya](/wiki/Himalaya "Himalaya") and [Hindukush](/wiki/Hindukush "Hindukush") mountains in the north, along the Silk Route, Indian influence was linked with Buddhism. [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") and [Khotan](/wiki/Khotan "Khotan") were direct heirs of Gangetic Buddhism, despite the difference in languages. Many Tibetan monks even used to know Sanskrit very well.[[50]](#cite_note-Fussman-2009-50) In Khotan the Ramayana was well cicrulated in Khotanese language, though the narrative is slightly different from the Gangetic version.[[51]](#cite_note-Puri-51) In [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan") and [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") many Buddhist monasteries were established. These countries were used as a kind of springboard for the monks who brought Indian Buddhist texts and images to China.[[50]](#cite_note-Fussman-2009-50) Further north, in the [Gobi Desert](/wiki/Gobi_Desert "Gobi Desert"), statues of [Ganesha](/wiki/Ganesha "Ganesha") and [Kartikeya](/wiki/Kartikeya "Kartikeya") were found alongside Buddhist imagery in the [Mogao Caves](/wiki/Mogao_Caves "Mogao Caves").[[51]](#cite_note-Puri-51)

### Cultural commonalities

#### Religion, mythology and folklore

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Rama%2C_Sita%2C_and_Laksmana_-_Museu_do_Oriente_-_Lisbon%2C_Portugal_-_DSC06731.JPG/250px-Rama%2C_Sita%2C_and_Laksmana_-_Museu_do_Oriente_-_Lisbon%2C_Portugal_-_DSC06731.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rama,_Sita,_and_Laksmana_-_Museu_do_Oriente_-_Lisbon,_Portugal_-_DSC06731.JPG)

[Rama](/wiki/Rama "Rama"), [Sita](/wiki/Sita "Sita") and [Lakshmana](/wiki/Lakshmana "Lakshmana") in [Thai shadow play](/wiki/Nang_Talung "Nang Talung")

* [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Indonesia "Hinduism in Indonesia") is practised by the majority of Bali's population.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The [Cham people](/wiki/Cham_people "Cham people") of Vietnam still practice Hinduism as well. Though officially Buddhist, many Thai, Khmer, and Burmese people also worship Hindu gods in a form of syncretism.
* [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") have had a large role in spreading Hinduism in Southeast Asia. Even today many monarchies such as the royal court of Thailand still have Hindu rituals performed for the King by Hindu Brahmins.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)
* [Garuda](/wiki/Garuda "Garuda"), a Hindu mythological figure, is present in the [coats of arms of Indonesia](/wiki/National_Emblem_of_Indonesia "National Emblem of Indonesia"), [Thailand](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Thailand "Coat of arms of Thailand") and [Ulaanbaatar](/wiki/Ulaanbaatar "Ulaanbaatar").
* [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai"), a fighting art that is the Thai version of the Hindu [Musti-yuddha](/wiki/Musti-yuddha "Musti-yuddha") style of martial art.
* [Kaharingan](/wiki/Kaharingan "Kaharingan"), an indigenous religion followed by the [Dayak people](/wiki/Dayak_people "Dayak people") of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), is categorised as a form of Hinduism in Indonesia.
* [Philippine mythology](/wiki/Philippine_mythology "Philippine mythology") includes the supreme god [Bathala](/wiki/Bathala "Bathala") and the concept of *[Diwata](/wiki/Anito "Anito")* and the still-current belief in *[Karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma")*—all derived from Hindu-Buddhist concepts.
* [Malay folklore](/wiki/Malay_folklore "Malay folklore") contains a rich number of Indian-influenced mythological characters, such as [Bidadari](/wiki/Bidadari "Bidadari"), [Jentayu](/wiki/Jatayu "Jatayu"), [Garuda](/wiki/Garuda "Garuda") and [Naga](/wiki/N%C4%81ga "Nāga").
* [Wayang](/wiki/Wayang "Wayang") shadow puppets and classical dance-dramas of [Indonesia](/wiki/Dance_in_Indonesia#The_Hindu-Buddhist_Era "Dance in Indonesia"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Dance_in_Cambodia "Dance in Cambodia"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Dance_in_Malaysia "Dance in Malaysia") and [Thailand](/wiki/Dance_in_Thailand "Dance in Thailand") took stories from episodes of *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")* and *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")*.

#### Caste system

Indians spread their religion to Southeast Asia, beginning the Hindu and Buddhist cultures there. They introduced the [caste system](/wiki/Caste_system_of_India "Caste system of India") to the region, especially to [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), Bali, [Madura](/wiki/Madura_Island "Madura Island"), and Sumatra. The adopted caste system was not as strict as in India, tempered to the local context.[[55]](#cite_note-Coedes-55) There are multiple similarities between the two caste systems such that both state that no one is equal within society and that everyone has his own place. It also promoted the upbringing of highly organized central states. Indians were still able to implement their religion, political ideas, literature, mythology, and art.[[55]](#cite_note-Coedes-55)

#### Architecture and monuments

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Prambanan_Complex_1.jpg/250px-Prambanan_Complex_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prambanan_Complex_1.jpg)

The 9th-century Shivaistic temple of [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") in [Central Java](/wiki/Central_Java "Central Java") near [Yogyakarta](/wiki/Yogyakarta "Yogyakarta"), the largest Hindu temple in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")

* The same style of [Hindu temple architecture](/wiki/Hindu_temple_architecture "Hindu temple architecture") was used in several ancient temples in Southeast Asia including [Angkor Wat](/wiki/Angkor_Wat "Angkor Wat"), which was dedicated to Hindu god [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") and is shown on the [flag of Cambodia](/wiki/Flag_of_Cambodia "Flag of Cambodia"), also [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") in Central Java, the largest Hindu temple in Indonesia, is dedicated to [Trimurti](/wiki/Trimurti "Trimurti") — [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva"), [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") and [Brahma](/wiki/Brahma "Brahma").
* [Borobudur](/wiki/Borobudur "Borobudur") in Central Java, Indonesia, is the world's largest Buddhist monument. It took shape of a giant stone [mandala](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala") crowned with [stupas](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa") and believed to be the combination of Indian-origin Buddhist ideas with the previous [megalithic](/wiki/Megalithic "Megalithic") tradition of native [Austronesian](/wiki/Austronesian_people "Austronesian people") [step pyramid](/wiki/Step_pyramid "Step pyramid").
* The minarets of 15th- to 16th-century mosques in Indonesia, such as the [Great Mosque of Demak](/wiki/Masjid_Agung_Demak "Masjid Agung Demak") and [Kudus mosque](/wiki/Menara_Kudus_Mosque "Menara Kudus Mosque") resemble those of [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit") Hindu temples.
* The [Batu Caves](/wiki/Batu_Caves "Batu Caves") in Malaysia are one of the most popular Hindu shrines outside India. It is the focal point of the annual [Thaipusam](/wiki/Thaipusam "Thaipusam") festival in Malaysia and attracts over 1.5 million pilgrims, making it one of the largest religious gatherings in history.[[56]](#cite_note-56)
* [Erawan Shrine](/wiki/Erawan_Shrine "Erawan Shrine"), dedicated to [Brahma](/wiki/Brahma_(Buddhism) "Brahma (Buddhism)"), is one of the most popular religious shrines in Thailand.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### Sport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/1-BISHNUPUR.jpg/250px-1-BISHNUPUR.jpg)](/wiki/File:1-BISHNUPUR.jpg)

[Atya patya](/wiki/Atya_patya "Atya patya"), a game of crossing a narrow field without being tagged.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Permainan_Hadang.jpg/120px-Permainan_Hadang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Permainan_Hadang.jpg)

Malaysian variant [galah panjang](/wiki/Galah_panjang "Galah panjang").

It is conjectured that certain [traditional Indian games](/wiki/Traditional_games_of_India "Traditional games of India") spread throughout Southeast Asia, as variations of Indian games such as [atya-patya](/wiki/Atya-patya "Atya-patya") and [gilli danda](/wiki/Gilli_danda "Gilli danda") can be found throughout the region.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Also, the Indonesian [hom pim pa](/wiki/Traditional_games_in_Indonesia#Hom_pim_pa "Traditional games in Indonesia") (a method of selecting players before a game) may involve the use of a Sanskrit phrase.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Linguistic influence

See also: [Indosphere](/wiki/Indosphere "Indosphere")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Character-sphere.png/250px-Character-sphere.png)](/wiki/File:Character-sphere.png)

A map of East, South and Southeast Asia. **Red** signifies current and historical (northern Vietnam) distribution of [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters"). **Green** signifies current and historical (Malaysia, Pakistan, the Maldives, Indonesia, the Philippines, and what is now the southern portion of Vietnam) distribution of [Indic scripts](/wiki/Brahmic_scripts "Brahmic scripts"). **Blue** signifies current and historical ([northeastern China](/wiki/Northeastern_China "Northeastern China")) use of non-Sinitic and non-Indic scripts.

Scholars like Sheldon Pollock have used the term *Sanskrit Cosmopolis* to describe the region and argued for millennium-long cultural exchanges without necessarily involving migration of peoples or colonisation. Pollock's 2006 book *The Language of the Gods in the World of Men* makes a case for studying the region as comparable with Latin Europe and argues that the Sanskrit language was its unifying element.

Scripts in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") discovered during the early centuries of the Common Era are the earliest known forms of writing to have extended all the way to Southeast Asia. Its gradual impact ultimately resulted in its widespread domain as a means of dialect which evident in regions, from Bangladesh to Cambodia, Malaysia and Thailand and additionally a few of the larger Indonesian islands. In addition, alphabets from languages spoken in Burmese, Thai, Laos, and Cambodia are variations formed off of Indian ideals that have localized the language.

Sanskrit and related languages have also influenced their [Tibeto-Burman](/wiki/Tibeto-Burman_languages "Tibeto-Burman languages")-speaking neighbors to the north through the spread of Buddhist texts in translation.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The spread of Buddhism to Tibet allowed many Sanskrit texts to survive only in Tibetan translation (in the [Tanjur](/wiki/Tanjur "Tanjur")). Buddhism was similarly introduced to China by [Mahayanist](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") missionaries mostly through translations of [Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit](/wiki/Buddhist_Hybrid_Sanskrit "Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit") and Classical Sanskrit texts, and many terms were [transliterated](/wiki/Transliterated "Transliterated")[[61]](#cite_note-sunnytantikumar736-61) directly and added to the Chinese vocabulary.

In Southeast Asia, languages such as [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language") contain many [loan words](/wiki/Loan_word "Loan word") from Sanskrit, as does [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language") to a lesser extent. For example, in Thai, [Rāvaṇa](/wiki/Ravana "Ravana"), the legendary emperor of [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), is called 'Thosakanth' which is derived from his Sanskrit name 'Daśakaṇṭha' ("having ten necks").

Many Sanskrit loanwords are also found in [Austronesian languages](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages"), such as [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") particularly the [old form](/wiki/Old_Javanese "Old Javanese") from which nearly half the vocabulary is derived from the language.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) Other Austronesian languages, such as [traditional Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"), [modern Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"), also derive [much of their vocabulary](/wiki/List_of_loan_words_in_Indonesian#From_Sanskrit "List of loan words in Indonesian") from Sanskrit, albeit to a lesser extent, with a large proportion of words being derived from [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"). Similarly, [Philippine languages](/wiki/Philippine_languages "Philippine languages") such as [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") have [many Sanskrit loanwords](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Tagalog#Sanskrit "List of loanwords in Tagalog").

A Sanskrit loanword encountered in many Southeast Asian languages is the word *[bhāṣā](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language")*, or spoken language, which is used to mean language in general, for example *bahasa* in Malay, Indonesian and [Tausug](/wiki/Tausug_language "Tausug language"), *basa* in Javanese, [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_language "Sundanese language"), and [Balinese](/wiki/Balinese_language "Balinese language"), *phasa* in Thai and Lao, *bhasa* in [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"), and *phiesa* in [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language").

### Literature

See also: [Literature about Southeast Asia](/wiki/Literature_about_Southeast_Asia "Literature about Southeast Asia") and [South Asian literature](/wiki/South_Asian_literature "South Asian literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Kakawin_ramayana_Or_14022_f2-4.jpg/330px-Kakawin_ramayana_Or_14022_f2-4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kakawin_ramayana_Or_14022_f2-4.jpg)

Pages of [Kakawin Ramayana](/wiki/Kakawin_Ramayana "Kakawin Ramayana"), the version of *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")* from Java and Bali

Scripts in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") discovered during the early centuries of the Common Era are the earliest known forms of writing to have extended all the way to Southeast Asia. Its gradual impact ultimately resulted in its widespread domain as a means of dialect which evident in regions, from Bangladesh to Cambodia, Malaysia and Thailand and additionally a few of the larger Indonesian islands. In addition, alphabets from languages spoken in Burmese, Thai, Laos, and Cambodia are variations formed off of Indian ideals that have localized the language.[[64]](#cite_note-Smith-1999-64)

The utilization of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") has been prevalent in all aspects of life including legal purposes. Sanskrit terminology and vernacular appears in ancient courts to establish procedures that have been structured by Indian models such as a system composed of a code of laws. The concept of legislation demonstrated through codes of law and organizations particularly the idea of "God King" was embraced by numerous rulers of Southeast Asia.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoedes196798-65) The rulers amid this time, for example, the Lin-I Dynasty of [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") once embraced the Sanskrit dialect and devoted sanctuaries to the Indian divinity Shiva. Many rulers following even viewed themselves as "reincarnations or descendants" of the Hindu gods. However once Buddhism began entering the nations, this practiced view was eventually altered.

### Linguistic commonalities

* In the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago"): [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") and [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") have absorbed a large number of [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") loanwords into their respective lexicons (see: [Sanskrit loan words in Indonesian](/wiki/List_of_loan_words_in_Indonesian#From_Sanskrit "List of loan words in Indonesian")). Many languages of native lowland [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipinos "Filipinos") such as [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), [Ilocano](/wiki/Ilocano_language "Ilocano language")[[66]](#cite_note-66) and [Visayan](/wiki/Visayan_languages "Visayan languages")[[67]](#cite_note-67) contain numerous [Sanskrit loanwords](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Tagalog#Sanskrit "List of loanwords in Tagalog").
* In [Mainland Southeast Asia](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia"): [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"), [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"), [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"), and [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language") language have absorbed a significant amount of Sanskrit as well as [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") words.
* Many [Indonesian names](/wiki/Indonesian_names#Sanskrit-derived_names "Indonesian names") have Sanskrit origin (e.g. [Dewi Sartika](/wiki/Dewi_Sartika "Dewi Sartika"), [Megawati Sukarnoputri](/wiki/Megawati_Sukarnoputri "Megawati Sukarnoputri"), [Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono](/wiki/Susilo_Bambang_Yudhoyono "Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono"), Teuku Wisnu).
* Southeast Asian languages are traditionally written with Indic alphabets and therefore have extra letters not pronounced in the local language, so that original Sanskrit spelling can be preserved. An example is how the name of the late King of Thailand, [Bhumibol Adulyadej](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej"), is spelled in Sanskrit as "Bhumibol"ภูมิพล, yet is pronounced in Thai as "Phumipon" พูมิพน using Thai-Sanskrit pronunciation rules since the original Sanskrit sounds do not exist in Thai. [[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Toponyms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Ayutthaya_2.jpg/250px-Ayutthaya_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ayutthaya_2.jpg)

Ruins of [Ayutthaya](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") in Thailand; Ayutthaya derives its name from the ancient Indian city of [Ayodhya](/wiki/Ayodhya_(Ramayana) "Ayodhya (Ramayana)"), which has had wide cultural significance

* [Suvarnabhumi](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi "Suvarnabhumi") is a toponym that has been historically associated with Southeast Asia. In Sanskrit, it means "The Land of Gold". Thailand's [Suvarnabhumi Airport](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi_Airport "Suvarnabhumi Airport") is named after this toponym.
* Several of Indonesian [toponyms](/wiki/Toponym "Toponym") have Indian parallel or origin, such as [Madura](/wiki/Madura "Madura") with [Mathura](/wiki/Mathura "Mathura"), [Serayu](/wiki/Serayu "Serayu") and [Sarayu river](/wiki/Sarayu_River_(Ayodhya) "Sarayu River (Ayodhya)"), [Semeru](/wiki/Semeru "Semeru") with [Sumeru](/wiki/Sumeru "Sumeru") mountain, [Kalingga](/wiki/Kalingga "Kalingga") from [Kalinga Kingdom](/wiki/Kalinga_(historical_region) "Kalinga (historical region)"), and [Ngayogyakarta](/wiki/Yogyakarta "Yogyakarta") from [Ayodhya](/wiki/Ayodhya_(Ramayana) "Ayodhya (Ramayana)").
* Siamese ancient city of [Ayutthaya](/wiki/Ayutthaya_(city) "Ayutthaya (city)") also derived from Ramayana's Ayodhya.
* Names of places could simply render their Sanskrit origin, such as [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), from Singapura (*Singha-pura* the "lion city"), [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") from *Jaya* and *kreta* ("complete victory").
* Some of the Indonesian regencies such as [Indragiri Hulu](/wiki/Indragiri_Hulu_Regency "Indragiri Hulu Regency") and [Indragiri Hilir](/wiki/Indragiri_Hilir_Regency "Indragiri Hilir Regency") derived from Indragiri River, Indragiri itself means "mountain of [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra")".
* Some Thai toponyms also often have Indian parallels or Sanskrit origin, although the spellings are adapted to the Siamese tongue, such as [Ratchaburi](/wiki/Ratchaburi "Ratchaburi") from *Raja-puri* ("king's city"), and [Nakhon Si Thammarat](/wiki/Nakhon_Si_Thammarat "Nakhon Si Thammarat") from *Nagara Sri Dharmaraja*.
* The tendency to use Sanskrit for modern [neologism](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") also continued to modern day. In 1962 Indonesia changed the colonial name of [New Guinean](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") city of Hollandia to [Jayapura](/wiki/Jayapura "Jayapura") ("glorious city"), Orange mountain range to [Jayawijaya Mountains](/wiki/Jayawijaya_Mountains "Jayawijaya Mountains").
* Malaysia named their new government seat as [Putrajaya](/wiki/Putrajaya "Putrajaya") ("prince of glory") in 1999.

## Indianization of Southeast Asia

Further information: [Austronesian maritime trade network](/wiki/Austronesian_maritime_trade_network "Austronesian maritime trade network"), [Maritime silk road](/wiki/Maritime_silk_road "Maritime silk road"), [Indian maritime history](/wiki/Indian_maritime_history "Indian maritime history"), [Indian Ocean trade](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade "Indian Ocean trade"), [Hinduism in Southeast Asia](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Southeast_Asia "Hinduism in Southeast Asia"), [Buddhism in Southeast Asia](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Southeast_Asia "Buddhism in Southeast Asia"), [Balinese Hinduism](/wiki/Balinese_Hinduism "Balinese Hinduism"), and [History of Indian influence on Southeast Asia](/wiki/History_of_Indian_influence_on_Southeast_Asia "History of Indian influence on Southeast Asia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Austronesian_maritime_trade_network_in_the_Indian_Ocean.png/330px-Austronesian_maritime_trade_network_in_the_Indian_Ocean.png)](/wiki/File:Austronesian_maritime_trade_network_in_the_Indian_Ocean.png)

[Austronesian](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") [proto-historic](/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade") and [historic](/wiki/Maritime_Silk_Road "Maritime Silk Road") maritime trade network in the Indian Ocean[[69]](#cite_note-Manguin-2016-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Hinduism_Expansion_in_Asia_2023.svg/250px-Hinduism_Expansion_in_Asia_2023.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hinduism_Expansion_in_Asia_2023.svg)

Hinduism expansion in Asia, from its heartland in Indian Subcontinent, to the rest of Asia, especially Southeast Asia, started circa 1st century marked with the establishment of early Hindu settlements and polities in Southeast Asia.

Indianization is different from direct colonialism in that these Indianized lands were not inhabited by organizations or state elements from the Indian subcontinent, with exceptions such as the [Chola invasions](/wiki/South-East_Asia_campaign_of_Rajendra_Chola_I "South-East Asia campaign of Rajendra Chola I") of medieval times. Instead, Indian cultural influence from trade routes and language use slowly permeated through Southeast Asia, making the traditions a part of the region. The interactions between [India](/wiki/India "India") and Southeast Asia were marked by waves of influence and dominance. At some points, the Indian culture solely found its way into the region, and at other points, the influence was used to take over.

Southeast Asia was now situated in the central area of convergence of the Indian and the East Asian maritime trade routes, the basis for economic and cultural growth. The earliest [Hindu kingdoms](/wiki/Hindu_king "Hindu king") emerged in Sumatra and Java, followed by mainland polities such as Funan and Champa. Adoption of Indian civilization elements and individual adaptation stimulated the emergence of centralized states and the development of highly organized societies. Ambitious local leaders realized the benefits of Hinduism and Indian methods of administration, culture, literature, etc. Rule in accord with universal moral principles, represented in the concept of the *[devaraja](/wiki/Devaraja "Devaraja")*, was more appealing than the Chinese concept of intermediaries.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

The earliest Hindu kingdoms emerged in Sumatra and Java, followed by mainland polities such as Funan and Champa. Adoption of Indian civilization elements and individual adaptation stimulated the emergence of centralized states and localized caste systems in Southeast Asia.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Theories of Indianization of Southeast Asia

Further information: [Indianization of Southeast Asia](/wiki/Indianization_of_Southeast_Asia "Indianization of Southeast Asia") and [History of Indian influence on Southeast Asia](/wiki/History_of_Indian_influence_on_Southeast_Asia "History of Indian influence on Southeast Asia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Borobudur_ship.JPG/250px-Borobudur_ship.JPG)](/wiki/File:Borobudur_ship.JPG)

A [Borobudur ship](/wiki/Borobudur_ship "Borobudur ship") from the 8th century, a large native outrigger trading vessels, possibly of the [Sailendra](/wiki/Sailendra "Sailendra") and [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") [thalassocracies](/wiki/Thalassocracies "Thalassocracies")

As conclusive evidence is missing, numerous Indianization theories of Southeast Asia have emerged since the early 20th century. The central question usually revolves around the main propagator of Indian institutional and cultural ideas in Southeast Asia.

Iron Age trade expansion caused regional [geostrategic](/wiki/Geostrategy "Geostrategy") remodeling. [Austronesian](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") sailors from [Island Southeast Asia](/wiki/Island_Southeast_Asia "Island Southeast Asia") first established contact and trade with [Southern India](/wiki/Southern_India "Southern India") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") as early as 500 BCE. This resulted in the introduction of Southeast Asian material culture (including [catamarans](/wiki/Catamaran "Catamaran"), [outrigger boats](/wiki/Outrigger_boat "Outrigger boat"), sewn-plank boats, and [paan](/wiki/Paan "Paan")) and [cultigens](/wiki/Cultigen "Cultigen") (like [coconuts](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut"), [sandalwood](/wiki/Sandalwood "Sandalwood"), [bananas](/wiki/Banana "Banana"), and [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane")) to South Asia; as well as connecting the material cultures of India and [China](/wiki/China "China"). These early Austronesian trade routes linking Island Southeast Asia with India also became the maritime aspect of the wider [spice trade](/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade") network, which were later also used by [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_people "Tamil people") and [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") maritime trade. The sustained contact between Southeast Asia and South Asia resulted in cultural exchange, in addition to the exchange of commodities.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[69]](#cite_note-Manguin-2016-69)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Another theory of the spread of Indianization that focuses on the caste of [Vaishya](/wiki/Vaishya "Vaishya") [traders](/wiki/Sadhaba "Sadhaba") and their role for spreading Indian culture and language into Southeast Asia through trade. There were many trade incentives that brought Vaishya traders to Southeast Asia, the most important of which was gold. During the 4th century C.E., when the first evidence of Indian trader in Southeast Asia, the Indian sub-continent was at a deficiency for gold due to extensive control of overland trade routes by the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). This made many Vaishya traders look to the seas to acquire new gold, of which Southeast Asia was abundant. However, the conclusion that Indianization was just spread through trade is insufficient, as Indianization permeated through all classes of Southeast Asian society, not just the merchant classes.[[79]](#cite_note-Lukas-2001-79)

Another theory states that Indianization spread through the warrior class of [Kshatriya](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya"). This hypothesis effectively explains state formation in Southeast Asia, as these warriors came with the intention of conquering the local peoples and establishing their own political power in the region. However, this theory hasn't attracted much interest from historians as there is very little literary evidence to support it.[[79]](#cite_note-Lukas-2001-79)

The most widely accepted theory for the spread of Indianization into Southeast Asia is through the class of [Brahman](/wiki/Brahman "Brahman") scholars. These Brahmans brought with them many of the Hindu religious and philosophical traditions and spread them to the elite classes of Southeast Asian polities. Once these traditions were adopted into the elite classes, it disseminated throughout all the lower classes, thus explaining the Indianization present in all classes of Southeast Asian society. Brahmans were also experts in art and architecture, and political affairs, thus explaining the adoption of many Indian style law codes and architecture into Southeast Asian society[[79]](#cite_note-Lukas-2001-79)

### Adaption and adoption

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Angkor_Wat_Aerial_View_Siem_Reap_Cambodia_2011.jpg/250px-Angkor_Wat_Aerial_View_Siem_Reap_Cambodia_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Angkor_Wat_Aerial_View_Siem_Reap_Cambodia_2011.jpg)

[Angkor Wat](/wiki/Angkor_Wat "Angkor Wat") in Cambodia is the largest Hindu temple in the world

It is unknown how immigration, interaction, and settlement took place, whether by key figures from India or through Southeast Asians visiting India who took elements of Indian culture back home. It is likely that Hindu and Buddhist traders, priests, and princes traveled to Southeast Asia from India in the first few centuries of the Common Era and eventually settled there. Strong impulse most certainly came from the region's ruling classes who invited Brahmans to serve at their courts as priests, astrologers and advisers.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Divinity and royalty were closely connected in these polities as Hindu rituals validated the powers of the monarch. Brahmans and priests from India proper played a key role in supporting ruling dynasties through exact rituals. Dynastic consolidation was the basis for more centralized kingdoms that emerged in Java, Sumatra, Cambodia, Burma, and along the central and south coasts of Vietnam from the 4th to 8th centuries.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Shiva_Temple_of_Prambanan_in_Java_Indonesia.jpg/250px-Shiva_Temple_of_Prambanan_in_Java_Indonesia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shiva_Temple_of_Prambanan_in_Java_Indonesia.jpg)

The 9th century Shiva temple in [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") compound, adorned with bas-reliefs of Ramayana, located near Yogyakarta, Indonesia

Art, architecture, rituals, and cultural elements such as the [Rāmāyaṇa](/wiki/R%C4%81m%C4%81ya%E1%B9%87a "Rāmāyaṇa") and the [Mahābhārata](/wiki/Mah%C4%81bh%C4%81rata "Mahābhārata") had been adopted and customized increasingly with a regional character. The caste system, although adopted, was never applied universally and reduced to serve for a selected group of nobles only.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Many struggle to date and determine when Indianizaton in Southeast Asia occurred because of the structures and ruins found that were similar to those in India.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

States such as [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya"), [Mataram](/wiki/Mataram_Kingdom "Mataram Kingdom"), [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit") and the [Khmer empire](/wiki/Khmer_empire "Khmer empire") had territorial continuity, resilient population and surplus economies that rivaled those in India itself. [Borobudur](/wiki/Borobudur "Borobudur") and [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") in Java and [Angkor](/wiki/Angkor "Angkor") in Cambodia are, apart from their grandeur, examples of a distinctly developed regional culture, style, and expression.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Southeast Asia is called [Suvarnabhumi](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi "Suvarnabhumi") or Sovannah Phoum – the golden land and Suvarnadvipa – the golden Islands in Sanskrit.[[86]](#cite_note-86) It was frequented by [traders](/wiki/Sadhaba "Sadhaba") from eastern India, particularly [Kalinga](/wiki/Kalinga_(historical_region) "Kalinga (historical region)"). Cultural and trading relations between the powerful [Chola dynasty](/wiki/Chola_dynasty "Chola dynasty") of [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India") and the Southeast Asian Hindu kingdoms led the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal") to be called "The Chola Lake", and the Chola attacks on Srivijaya in the 10th century CE are the sole example of military attacks by Indian rulers against Southeast Asia. The [Pala dynasty](/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire") of [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), which controlled the heartland of Buddhist India, maintained close economic, cultural and religious ties, particularly with Srivijaya.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

### Religion, authority and legitimacy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Ramayana_Bali_Ubud_1.jpg/330px-Ramayana_Bali_Ubud_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ramayana_Bali_Ubud_1.jpg)

Balinese Ramayana dance drama, performed in Sarasvati Garden in [Ubud](/wiki/Ubud "Ubud").

The pre-Indic political and social systems in Southeast Asia were marked by a relative indifference towards lineage descent. Hindu God kingship enabled rulers to supersede loyalties, forge cosmopolitan polities and the worship of Shiva and Vishnu was combined with ancestor worship, so that Khmer, Javanese, and Cham rulers claimed semi-divine status as descendants of a God. Hindu traditions, especially the relationship to the sacrality of the land and social structures, are inherent in Hinduism's transnational features. The epic traditions of the Mahābhārata and the Rāmāyaṇa further legitimized a ruler identified with a God who battled and defeated the wrong doers that threaten the ethical order of the world.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

Hinduism does not have a single historical founder, a centralized imperial authority in India proper nor a bureaucratic structure, thus ensuring relative religious independence for the individual ruler. It also allows for multiple forms of divinity, centered upon the [Trimurti](/wiki/Trimurti "Trimurti") the triad of Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva, the deities responsible for the creation, preservation, and destruction of the universe.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

The effects of Hinduism and Buddhism applied a tremendous impact on the many civilizations inhabiting Southeast Asia which significantly provided some structure to the composition of written traditions. An essential factor for the spread and adaptation of these religions originated from trading systems of the third and fourth century.[[64]](#cite_note-Smith-1999-64) In order to spread the message of these religions Buddhist monks and Hindu priests joined mercantile classes in the quest to share their religious and cultural values and beliefs. Along the Mekong delta, evidence of Indianized religious models can be observed in communities labeled Funan. There can be found the earliest records engraved on a rock in Vocanh.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The engravings consist of Buddhist archives and a south Indian scripts are written in Sanskrit that have been dated to belong to the early half of the third century. Indian religion was profoundly absorbed by local cultures that formed their own distinctive variations of these structures in order to reflect their own ideals.

[Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa"), [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati"), [Funan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Funan "Kingdom of Funan"), [Gangga Negara](/wiki/Gangga_Negara "Gangga Negara"), [Kadaram](/wiki/Early_history_of_Kedah "Early history of Kedah"), [Kalingga](/wiki/Kalingga "Kalingga"), [Kutai](/wiki/Kutai "Kutai"), [Langkasuka](/wiki/Langkasuka "Langkasuka"), [Pagan](/wiki/Pagan_Kingdom "Pagan Kingdom"), [Pan Pan](/wiki/Pan_Pan_(kingdom) "Pan Pan (kingdom)"), [Po-ni](/wiki/History_of_Brunei "History of Brunei"), and [Tarumanagara](/wiki/Tarumanagara "Tarumanagara") had by the 1st to 4th centuries CE adopted Hinduism's cosmology and rituals, the *[devaraja](/wiki/Devaraja "Devaraja")* concept of kingship, and Sanskrit as official writing. Despite the fundamental cultural integration, these kingdoms were autonomous in their own right and functioned independently.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Waning of Indianization

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Map-of-southeast-asia_900_CE.png/250px-Map-of-southeast-asia_900_CE.png)](/wiki/File:Map-of-southeast-asia_900_CE.png)

Map of South-east Asia c. 900 AD, showing the [Khmer Empire](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire") in red, [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") in green, and [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa") in yellow.

#### Khmer Kingdom

Not only did Indianization change many cultural and political aspects, but it also changed the spiritual realm as well, creating a type of Northern Culture which began in the early 14th century, prevalent for its rapid decline in the Indian kingdoms. The decline of Hindu kingdoms and spark of Buddhist kingdoms led to the formation of orthodox Sinhalese Buddhism and is a key factor leading to the decline of Indianization. Sukhothai and Ceylon are the prominent characters who formulated the center of Buddhism and thus became more popularized over Hinduism.[[55]](#cite_note-Coedes-55)

#### Rise of Islam

Not only was the spark of Buddhism the driving force for Indianization coming to an end, but Islamic influence took over as well in the midst of the thirteenth century to trump the Hindu kingdoms. In the process of Islam coming to the traditional Hindu kingdoms, trade was heavily practiced and the now Islamized Indians started becoming merchants all over Southeast Asia.[[55]](#cite_note-Coedes-55) Moreover, as trade became more saturated in the Southeast Asian regions wherein Indianization once persisted, the regions had become more Muslim populated. This so-called Islamic control has spanned to many of the trading centers across the regions of Southeast Asia, including one of the most dominant centers, Malacca, and has therefore stressed a widespread rise of Islamization.[[55]](#cite_note-Coedes-55)

## Indianized kingdoms of Southeast Asia

Further information: [List of Hindu empires and dynasties](/wiki/List_of_Hindu_empires_and_dynasties "List of Hindu empires and dynasties")

### Mainland kingdoms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Dancing_Shiva_10th_c.jpg/250px-Dancing_Shiva_10th_c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dancing_Shiva_10th_c.jpg)

The 10th-century [tympanum](/wiki/Tympanum_(architecture) "Tympanum (architecture)") of the dancing [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") in [Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa"), Vietnam

* **[Funan](/wiki/Funan "Funan")**: Funan was a polity that encompassed the southernmost part of the [Indochinese peninsula](/wiki/Indochina "Indochina") during the 1st to 6th centuries. The name *Funan* is not found in any texts of local origin from the period, and so is considered an [exonym](/wiki/Exonym "Exonym") based on the accounts of two Chinese diplomats, [Kang Tai](/wiki/Kang_Tai "Kang Tai") and Zhu Ying who sojourned there in the mid-3rd century CE.[[92]](#cite_note-92): 24 It is not known what name the people of Funan gave to their polity. Some scholars believe ancient Chinese scholars transcribed the word Funan from a word related to the Khmer word bnaṃ or vnaṃ (modern: phnoṃ, meaning "mountain"); while others thought that Funan may not be a transcription at all, rather it meant what it says in Chinese, meaning something like "Pacified South". Centered at the lower [Mekong](/wiki/Mekong "Mekong"),[[93]](#cite_note-93) Funan is noted as the oldest [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") culture in this region, which suggests prolonged socio-economic interaction with India and maritime trading partners of the [Indosphere](/wiki/Indosphere "Indosphere").[[7]](#cite_note-Stark-1999-7) Cultural and religious ideas had reached Funan via the [Indian Ocean trade](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade "Indian Ocean trade") route. Trade with India had commenced well before 500 BC as [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") had not yet replaced [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali").[[7]](#cite_note-Stark-1999-7) Funan's language has been determined as to have been an early form of Khmer and its written form was Sanskrit.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Cat_Tien_large_lingam.jpg/250px-Cat_Tien_large_lingam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cat_Tien_large_lingam.jpg)

Stone lingam found in [Cát Tiên](/wiki/C%C3%A1t_Ti%C3%AAn_archaeological_site "Cát Tiên archaeological site"), southern Vietnam

* **[Chenla](/wiki/Chenla_Kingdom "Chenla Kingdom")** was the successor polity of Funan that existed from around the late 6th century until the early 9th century in Indochina, preceding the [Khmer Empire](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire"). Like its predecessor, Chenla occupied a strategic position where the maritime trade routes of the [Indosphere](/wiki/Indosphere "Indosphere") and the [East Asian cultural sphere](/wiki/East_Asian_cultural_sphere "East Asian cultural sphere") converged, resulting in prolonged socio-economic and cultural influence, along with the adoption of the Sanskrit [epigraphic](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") system of the south Indian [Pallava dynasty](/wiki/Pallava_dynasty "Pallava dynasty") and [Chalukya dynasty](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty").[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) Chenla's first ruler Vīravarman adopted the idea of divine kingship and deployed the concept of [Harihara](/wiki/Harihara "Harihara"), the [syncretistic](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") Hindu "god that embodied multiple conceptions of power". His successors continued this tradition, thus obeying the code of conduct [Manusmṛti](/wiki/Manusm%E1%B9%9Bti "Manusmṛti"), the *Laws of Manu* for the [Kshatriya](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya") warrior caste and conveying the idea of political and religious authority.[[6]](#cite_note-Lavy-2003-6)
* **[Langkasuka](/wiki/Langkasuka "Langkasuka")**: Langkasuka (-*langkha* [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") for "resplendent land" -*sukkha* of "bliss") was an ancient [Hindu kingdom](/wiki/Hindu_king "Hindu king") located in the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"). The kingdom, along with the Old Kedah settlement, are probably the earliest territorial footholds founded on the Malay Peninsula. According to tradition, the founding of the kingdom happened in the 2nd century; [Malay](/wiki/Malay_people "Malay people") legends claim that Langkasuka was founded at [Kedah](/wiki/Kedah "Kedah"), and later moved to [Pattani](/wiki/Pattani_province "Pattani province").[[97]](#cite_note-97)
* **[Champa](/wiki/Champa "Champa")**: The kingdoms of Champa controlled what is now south and central [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"). The earliest kingdom, [Lâm Ấp](/wiki/L%C3%A2m_%E1%BA%A4p "Lâm Ấp") was described by Chinese sources around 192. CE The dominant religion was [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and the culture was heavily influenced by India. By the late fifteenth century, the Vietnamese – proponents of the [Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere") – had eradicated the last remaining traces of the once powerful maritime kingdom of Champa.[[98]](#cite_note-98) The last surviving [Chams](/wiki/Cham_(Asia) "Cham (Asia)") began their [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") in 1471, many re-settling in [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_people "Khmer people") territory.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)
* **[Kambuja](/wiki/Khmer_empire "Khmer empire")**: The [Khmer Empire](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire") was established by the early 9th century in a mythical initiation and [consecration](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration") ceremony by founder [Jayavarman II](/wiki/Jayavarman_II "Jayavarman II") at Mount Kulen (Mount Mahendra) in 802 CE[[101]](#cite_note-101) A succession of powerful sovereigns, continuing the [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") [devaraja](/wiki/Devaraja "Devaraja") tradition, reigned over the classical era of Khmer civilization until the 11th century. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") was then introduced temporarily into royal religious practice, with discontinuities and decentralisation resulting in subsequent removal.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The [royal chronology](/wiki/List_of_kings_of_Cambodia "List of kings of Cambodia") ended in the 14th century. During this period of the Khmer empire, societal functions of [administration](/wiki/Administration_(government) "Administration (government)"), agriculture, architecture, [hydrology](/wiki/Hydrology "Hydrology"), [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics"), [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning"), literature and [the arts](/wiki/Art "Art") saw an unprecedented degree of development, refinement and accomplishment from the distinct expression of Hindu cosmology.[[103]](#cite_note-103)
* **[Mon kingdoms](/wiki/Mon_kingdoms "Mon kingdoms")**: From the 9th century until the abrupt end of the [Hanthawaddy kingdom](/wiki/Hanthawaddy_kingdom "Hanthawaddy kingdom") in 1539, the Mon kingdoms ([Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati"), [Hariphunchai](/wiki/Hariphunchai "Hariphunchai"), [Pegu](/wiki/Pegu "Pegu")) were notable for facilitating Indianized cultural exchange in lower Burma, in particular by having strong ties with Sri Lanka.[[104]](#cite_note-104)
* **[Sukhothai](/wiki/Sukhothai_Kingdom "Sukhothai Kingdom")**: The first [Tai peoples](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") to gain independence from the Khmer Empire and start their own kingdom in the 13th century. Sukhothai was a precursor for the [Ayutthaya Kingdom](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") and the Kingdom of Siam. Though ethnically Thai, the Sukhothai kingdom in many ways was a continuation of the Buddhist Mon-Dvaravati civilizations, as well as the neighboring Khmer Empire.[[61]](#cite_note-sunnytantikumar736-61)[[105]](#cite_note-105)

### Island kingdoms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Avalokite%C3%A7vara%2C_Malayu_Srivijaya_style.jpg/250px-Avalokite%C3%A7vara%2C_Malayu_Srivijaya_style.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avalokite%C3%A7vara,_Malayu_Srivijaya_style.jpg)

Golden [Avalokiteśvara](/wiki/Avalokite%C5%9Bvara "Avalokiteśvara") from [Malayu](/wiki/Melayu_Kingdom "Melayu Kingdom")-[Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya"), Indonesia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Durga_Mahisasuramardini_Prambanan.jpg/250px-Durga_Mahisasuramardini_Prambanan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durga_Mahisasuramardini_Prambanan.jpg)

Statue [Durga](/wiki/Durga "Durga") dated to the 9th-century [Mataram](/wiki/Mataram_Kingdom "Mataram Kingdom") from Central Java

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Pura_Ulandanu_Temple%2C_Batur%2C_Bali%2C_Indonesia_02.JPG/250px-Pura_Ulandanu_Temple%2C_Batur%2C_Bali%2C_Indonesia_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pura_Ulandanu_Temple,_Batur,_Bali,_Indonesia_02.JPG)

[Ganesha](/wiki/Ganesha "Ganesha") shrine in Bali, a widely present custom from [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit")

* **[Salakanagara](/wiki/Salakanagara "Salakanagara")**: Salakanagara kingdom is the first historically recorded Indianized kingdom in Western Java, established by an Indian trader after marrying a local Sundanese princess. This Kingdom existed between 130 and 362 CE.[[106]](#cite_note-106)
* **[Tarumanagara](/wiki/Tarumanagara "Tarumanagara")** was an early Sundanese Indianized kingdom, located not far from modern Jakarta, and according to Tugu inscription ruler Purnavarman apparently built a canal that changed the course of the Cakung River, and drained a coastal area for agriculture and settlement. In his inscriptions, Purnavarman associated himself with Vishnu, and Brahmins ritually secured the hydraulic project.
* **[Kalingga](/wiki/Kalingga_Kingdom "Kalingga Kingdom")**: Kalingga (Javanese: Karajan Kalingga) was the 6th century Indianized kingdom on the north coast of Central Java, Indonesia. It was the earliest Hindu-Buddhist kingdom in Central Java, and together with Kutai and Tarumanagara are the oldest kingdoms in Indonesian history.
* **[Malayu](/wiki/Melayu_Kingdom "Melayu Kingdom")** was a classical Southeast Asian kingdom. The primary sources for much of the information on the kingdom are the New History of the Tang, and the memoirs of the Chinese Buddhist monk Yijing who visited in 671 CE, and states that it was "absorbed" by Srivijaya by 692 CE, but had "broken away" by the end of the eleventh century according to Chao Jukua. The exact location of the kingdom is the subject of studies among historians.
* **[Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya")**: From the 7th to 13th centuries Srivijaya, a maritime empire centered on the island of [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") in Indonesia, had adopted Mahayana and Vajrayana Buddhism under a line of rulers from Dapunta Hyang Sri Jayanasa to the [Sailendras](/wiki/Sailendra "Sailendra"). A stronghold of [Vajrayana](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") Buddhism, Srivijaya attracted pilgrims and scholars from other parts of Asia. I Ching reports that the kingdom was home to more than a thousand Buddhist scholars. A notable Buddhist scholar of local origin, [Dharmakirti](/wiki/Dharmakirti "Dharmakirti"), taught Buddhist philosophy in Srivijaya and [Nalanda](/wiki/Nalanda "Nalanda") (in India), and was the teacher of [Atisha](/wiki/Atisha "Atisha"). Most of the time, this Buddhist [Malay](/wiki/Ethnic_Malay "Ethnic Malay") empire enjoyed cordial relationship with China and the [Pala Empire](/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire") in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), and the 860 CE [Nalanda inscription](/wiki/Nalanda_inscription "Nalanda inscription") records that Maharaja [Balaputra](/wiki/Balaputra "Balaputra") dedicated a monastery at [Nalanda](/wiki/Nalanda "Nalanda") university near Pala territory. The Srivijaya kingdom ceased to exist in the 13th century due to various factors, including the expansion of the Javanese, Singhasari, and Majapahit empires.[[107]](#cite_note-107)
* **[Tambralinga](/wiki/Tambralinga "Tambralinga")** was an ancient kingdom located on the Malay Peninsula that at one time came under the influence of Srivijaya. The name had been forgotten until scholars recognized Tambralinga as Nagara Sri Dharmaraja (Nakhon Si Thammarat). Early records are scarce but its duration is estimated to range from the seventh to the fourteenth century. Tambralinga first sent tribute to the emperor of the Tang dynasty in 616 CE. In Sanskrit, Tambra means "red" and linga means "symbol", typically representing the divine energy of Shiva.
* **[Mataram](/wiki/Mataram_Kingdom "Mataram Kingdom")**: The Mataram Kingdom flourished between the 8th and 11th centuries. It was first centered in central Java before moving later to east Java. This kingdom produced numbers of Hindu-Buddhist temples in Java, including [Borobudur](/wiki/Borobudur "Borobudur") Buddhist mandala and the [Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") [Trimurti](/wiki/Trimurti "Trimurti") Hindu temple dedicated mainly to [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva"). The [Sailendras](/wiki/Sailendra "Sailendra") were the ruling family of this kingdom at an earlier stage in central Java, before being replaced by the [Isyana Dynasty](/wiki/Isyana_Dynasty "Isyana Dynasty").
* **[Kadiri](/wiki/Kediri_Kingdom "Kediri Kingdom")**: In the 10th century, Mataram challenged the supremacy of Srivijaya, resulting in the destruction of the Mataram capital by Srivijaya early in the 11th century. Restored by King [Airlangga](/wiki/Airlangga "Airlangga") (c. 1020–1050), the kingdom split on his death; the new state of Kediri, in eastern Java, became the centre of Javanese culture for the next two centuries, spreading its influence to the eastern parts of Southeast Asia. The spice trade was now becoming increasingly important, as demand from European countries grew. Before they learned to keep sheep and cattle alive in the winter, they had to eat salted meat, made palatable by the addition of spices. One of the main sources was the [Maluku Islands](/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands") (or "Spice Islands") in Indonesia, and so Kediri became a strong trading nation.
* **[Singhasari](/wiki/Singhasari "Singhasari")**: In the 13th century, however, the Kediri dynasty was overthrown by a revolution, and [Singhasari](/wiki/Singhasari "Singhasari") arose in east Java. The domains of this new state expanded under the rule of its warrior-king [Kertanegara](/wiki/Kertanegara "Kertanegara"). He was killed by a prince of the previous Kediri dynasty, who then established the last great Hindu-Javanese kingdom, [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit"). By the middle of the 14th century, Majapahit controlled most of Java, Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula, part of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), the southern Celebes and the Moluccas. It also exerted considerable influence on the mainland.
* **[Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit")**: The Majapahit empire, centered in East Java, succeeded the [Singhasari](/wiki/Singhasari "Singhasari") empire and flourished in the Indonesian archipelago between the 13th and 15th centuries. Noted for their naval expansion, the [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people") spanned west–east from Lamuri in [Aceh](/wiki/Aceh "Aceh") to Wanin in [Papua](/wiki/West_Papua_(province) "West Papua (province)"). Majapahit was one of the last and greatest Hindu empires in [Maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia"). Most of [Balinese](/wiki/Bali "Bali") Hindu culture, traditions and civilisations were derived from Majapahit legacy. A large number of Majapahit nobles, priests, and artisans found their home in Bali after the decline of Majapahit to [Demak Sultanate](/wiki/Demak_Sultanate "Demak Sultanate").
* **[Galuh](/wiki/Galuh_Kingdom "Galuh Kingdom")** was an ancient Hindu kingdom in the eastern Tatar Pasundan (now west Java province and Banyumasan region of central Java province), Indonesia. It was established following the collapse of the Tarumanagara kingdom around the 7th century. Traditionally the kingdom of Galuh was associated with the eastern Priangan cultural region, around the [Citanduy](/wiki/Citanduy "Citanduy") and Cimanuk rivers, with its territory spanning from Citarum river on the west, to the Pamali (present-day Brebes river) and Serayu rivers on the east. Its capital was located in Kawali, near present-day Ciamis city.
* **[Sunda](/wiki/Sunda_Kingdom "Sunda Kingdom")**: The Kingdom of Sunda was a Hindu kingdom located in western Java from 669 CE to around 1579 CE, covering the area of present-day Banten, Jakarta, West Java, and the western part of Central Java. According to primary historical records, the Bujangga Manik manuscript, the eastern border of the Sunda Kingdom was the Pamali River (Ci Pamali, the present day Brebes River) and the Serayu River (Ci Sarayu) in Central Java.

## Indianized kingdoms of Southwest Asia

See also: [Hindu and Buddhist heritage of Afghanistan](/wiki/Hindu_and_Buddhist_heritage_of_Afghanistan "Hindu and Buddhist heritage of Afghanistan") and [Muslim conquests of Afghanistan](/wiki/Muslim_conquests_of_Afghanistan "Muslim conquests of Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Corinthian_Capital_with_Sun_God_Surya_Riding_a_Chariot_%28Quadriga%29_Gandhara_100-200_CE.jpg/250px-Corinthian_Capital_with_Sun_God_Surya_Riding_a_Chariot_%28Quadriga%29_Gandhara_100-200_CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corinthian_Capital_with_Sun_God_Surya_Riding_a_Chariot_(Quadriga)_Gandhara_100-200_CE.jpg)

[Surya](/wiki/Surya "Surya") sitting on a Corinthian chariot from [ancient Afghanistan](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara")

The eastern regions of Afghanistan were considered regionally as parts of India and were also politically ruled by the [Mauryan Empire](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire"). Buddhism and Hinduism held sway over the region until the Muslim conquest.[[108]](#cite_note-108) Kabul and Zabulistan which housed Buddhism and other [Indian religions](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions"), offered stiff resistance to the Muslim advance for two centuries, with the [Kabul Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") and [Zunbils](/wiki/Zunbils "Zunbils") remaining unconquered until the [Saffarid](/wiki/Saffarid "Saffarid") and [Ghaznavid](/wiki/Ghaznavid "Ghaznavid") conquests.[[109]](#cite_note-109) The significance of the realm of [Zun](/wiki/Zun "Zun") and its rulers Zunbils had laid in them blocking the path of Arabs in invading the [Indus Valley](/wiki/Indus_Valley "Indus Valley").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

According to historian [André Wink](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Wink "André Wink"), "In southern and eastern Afghanistan, the regions of [Zamindawar](/wiki/Zamindawar "Zamindawar") (Zamin I Datbar or land of the justice giver, the classical [Arachosia](/wiki/Arachosia "Arachosia")) and [Zabulistan](/wiki/Zabulistan "Zabulistan") or [Zabul](/wiki/Zabul "Zabul") (Jabala, [Kapisha](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kapisa "Kingdom of Kapisa"), Kia pi shi) and [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul"), the Arabs were effectively opposed for more than two centuries, from 643 to 870 AD, by the indigenous rulers the [Zunbils](/wiki/Zunbil "Zunbil") and the related [Kabul-Shahs](/wiki/Turk_Shahi "Turk Shahi") of the dynasty which became known as the Buddhist-Shahi. With [Makran](/wiki/Makran "Makran") and [Baluchistan](/wiki/Baluchistan,_Pakistan "Baluchistan, Pakistan") and much of Sindh this area can be reckoned to belong to the cultural and political frontier zone between India and [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia")."[[111]](#cite_note-Wink-1996-111) He also wrote, "It is clear however that in the seventh to ninth centuries the Zunbils and their kinsmen the Kabulshahs ruled over a predominantly Indian rather than a Persianate realm. The Arab geographers, in effect, commonly speak of 'that king of al-Hind ... (who) bore the title of Zunbil."[[111]](#cite_note-Wink-1996-111)

Archaeological sites such as the 8th-century [Tapa Sardar](/wiki/Tapa_Sardar "Tapa Sardar") and Gardez show a blend of Buddhism with strong [Shaivst](/wiki/Shaivism "Shaivism") iconography.[[112]](#cite_note-112) Around 644 CE, the Chinese travelling monk [Xuanzang](/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang") made an account of Zabul (which he called by its Sanskrit name *Jaguda*), which he describes as mainly pagan, though also respecting [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") Buddhism, which although in the minority had the support of its royals. In terms of other cults, the god Śuna,[[113]](#cite_note-113) is described to be the prime deity of the country.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

The Caliph [Al-Ma'mun](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun "Al-Ma'mun") (r. 813–833 A.D.) led the last Arab expeditions on Kabul and Zabul, after which the long-drawn conflict ended with the dissolution of the empire. Rutbil were made to pay double the tribute to the Caliph.[[115]](#cite_note-115) The king of Kabul was captured by him and converted to Islam.[[116]](#cite_note-116) The last Zunbil was killed by [Ya'qub bin al-Layth](/wiki/Ya%27qub_ibn_al-Layth_al-Saffar "Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar") along with his former overlord Salih b. al-Nadr in 865.[[117]](#cite_note-117) Meanwhile, the Hindu Shahi of Kabul were defeated under [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni").[[118]](#cite_note-118) Indian soldiers were a part of the Ghaznavid army, [Baihaki](/wiki/Abu%27l-Fadl_Bayhaqi "Abu'l-Fadl Bayhaqi") mentioned Hindu officers employed by [Ma'sud](/wiki/Ma%27sud_I_of_Ghazni "Ma'sud I of Ghazni").[[119]](#cite_note-119) The 14th-century scholar Muslim scholar [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta") described the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") as meaning "slayer of Indians", because large numbers of slaves brought from [India](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") died from its treacherous weather.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

### Zabulistan

[Zabulistan](/wiki/Zabulistan "Zabulistan"), a historical region in southern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") roughly corresponding to the modern provinces of [Zabul](/wiki/Zabul_Province "Zabul Province") and [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni_Province "Ghazni Province"),[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122) was a collection of loose suzerains of the Hindu rulers when it fell to the [Turk Shahis](/wiki/Turk_Shahi "Turk Shahi") in the 7th century, though the suzerainty continued up to the 11th century. The Hindu kingdom of Kapisha had split up as its western part formed a separate state called the kingdom of Zabul. It was a family division because there were consanguineous and political relationships between the states of Kabul and Zabul.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

The [Zunbils](/wiki/Zunbil "Zunbil"), a royal dynasty south of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") in present-day southern Afghanistan region, worshiped the Zhuna, possibly a [sun god](/wiki/Solar_deity "Solar deity") connected to the Hindu god Surya and is sometimes referred to as Zoor or Zoon. He is represented with flames radiating from his head on coins. Statues were adorned with gold and used rubies for eyes. [Huen Tsang](/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang") calls him "sunagir".[[124]](#cite_note-124) It has been linked with the Hindu god [Aditya](/wiki/Surya "Surya") at [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan"), [pre-Buddhist religious](/wiki/Bon "Bon") and [kingship practices](/wiki/Imperial_cult "Imperial cult") of [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") as well as [Shaivism](/wiki/Shaivism "Shaivism").[[125]](#cite_note-125) His shrine lay on a sacred mountain in Zamindawar. Originally it appears to have been brought there by [Hepthalites](/wiki/Hepthalite "Hepthalite"), displacing an earlier god on the same site. Parallels have been noted with the pre-Buddhist monarchy of Tibet, next to [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrian "Zoroastrian") influence on its ritual. Whatever its origins, it was certainly superimposed on a mountain and on a pre-existing mountain god while merging with Shaiva doctrines of worship.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

### Buddhist Turk Shahi dynasty of Kabul

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Mes_Aynak_stupa.jpg/250px-Mes_Aynak_stupa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mes_Aynak_stupa.jpg)

Newly excavated Buddhist [stupa](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa") at [Mes Aynak](/wiki/Mes_Aynak "Mes Aynak") in Logar Province. Similar stupas have been discovered in neighboring [Ghazni Province](/wiki/Ghazni_Province "Ghazni Province"), including in the northern [Samangan Province](/wiki/Samangan_Province#Cultural_heritage "Samangan Province").
