The area had been under the rule of the [Turk Shahi](/wiki/Turk_Shahi "Turk Shahi") who took over the rule of Kabul in the seventh century and later were attacked by the Arabs.[[127]](#cite_note-127) The Turk Shahi dynasty was [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") and were followed by a [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") dynasty shortly before the Saffarid conquest in 870 A.D.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

The Turk Shahi were a Buddhist [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") dynasty that ruled from Kabul and [Kapisa](/wiki/Kapisa_(city) "Kapisa (city)") in the 7th to 9th centuries. They replaced the [Nezak](/wiki/Nezak "Nezak") – the last dynasty of Bactrian rulers. [Kabulistan](/wiki/Kabulistan "Kabulistan") was the heartland of the Turk Shahi domain, which at times included [Zabulistan](/wiki/Zabulistan "Zabulistan") and [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara").[[129]](#cite_note-129) The last Shahi ruler of Kabul, Lagaturman, was deposed by a [Brahmin](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") minister, possibly named Vakkadeva,[[130]](#cite_note-W._Macdowall_1968,_pp._189-224-130)[[131]](#cite_note-Raizada_Harichand_Vaid_pp._83-131)[[132]](#cite_note-Tabaqat-i-Nasiri'_p._82-132) in c. 850, signaling the end of the Buddhist Turk Shahi dynasty, and the beginning of the [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") dynasty from Kabul.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

### Hindu Shahis

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/AMB_Temple_in_Soon_Sakasar_Valley_by_Usman_Ghani.JPG/250px-AMB_Temple_in_Soon_Sakasar_Valley_by_Usman_Ghani.JPG)](/wiki/File:AMB_Temple_in_Soon_Sakasar_Valley_by_Usman_Ghani.JPG)

The [Amb Hindu Temple complex](/wiki/Amb_Temples "Amb Temples") was built between the 7th and 9th centuries CE during the reign of the Hindu Shahi Empire[[134]](#cite_note-134)

The Hindu Shahi (850–1026 CE) was a [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") dynasty that held sway over the [Kabul Valley](/wiki/Kabul_Valley "Kabul Valley"), [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") (modern-day north-west Pakistan and north-east Afghanistan), and present-day northwestern India, during the [early medieval period in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Medieval_India "Medieval India"). They succeeded the [Turk Shahis](/wiki/Turk_Shahi "Turk Shahi"). There were two successive dynasties in [Kabul Valley](/wiki/Kabul_Valley "Kabul Valley") and [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") – the [Kshatriya](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya") dynasty and the [Brahmana dynasty](/w/index.php?title=Brahmana_dynasty&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brahmana dynasty (page does not exist)") which replaced it.[[135]](#cite_note-Samad2011-135) Both used the title of *Shahi*. Details about these rulers have been assembled from [chronicles](/wiki/Chronicle "Chronicle"), coins and stone inscriptions by researchers as no consolidated account of their history has become available.[[135]](#cite_note-Samad2011-135) In 1973, Historian Yogendra Mishra proposed that according to [Rajatarangini](/wiki/Rajatarangini "Rajatarangini"), Hindu Shahis were [Kshatriyas](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya").[[136]](#cite_note-136)

According to available inscriptions following are the names of Hindu Shahi kings: Vakkadeva, Kamalavarman, Bhimadeva, [Jayapala](/wiki/Jayapala "Jayapala"), [Anandapala](/wiki/Anandapala "Anandapala"), [Trilochanapala](/wiki/Trilochanapala "Trilochanapala") and Bhimpala.[[130]](#cite_note-W._Macdowall_1968,_pp._189-224-130)[[131]](#cite_note-Raizada_Harichand_Vaid_pp._83-131)[[132]](#cite_note-Tabaqat-i-Nasiri'_p._82-132)

* **Vakkadeva:** According to *The Mazare Sharif Inscription of the Time of the Shahi Ruler Veka*, recently discovered from northern Afghanistan and reported by the Taxila Institute of Asian Civilisations, [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"), [Veka](/wiki/Veka "Veka") (sic.) conquered northern region of Afghanistan 'with eightfold forces' and ruled there. He established a [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") temple there which was inaugurated by *[Parimaha Maitya](/w/index.php?title=Parimaha_Maitya&action=edit&redlink=1 "Parimaha Maitya (page does not exist)")* (the Great Minister).[[137]](#cite_note-137) He also issued copper coins of the Elephant and Lion type with the legend Shri Vakkadeva. Nine principal issues of Bull and Horseman silver coins and only one issue of corresponding copper coins of Spalapatideva have become available. As many as five Elephant and Lion type of copper coins of Shri Vakkadeva are available and curiously the copper issues of Vakka are *contemporaneous* with the silver issues of Spalapati.[[130]](#cite_note-W._Macdowall_1968,_pp._189-224-130)
* **Kamalavarman:** During the reign of Kamalavarman, the Saffarid rule weakened precipitately and ultimately Sistan became a part of the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire"). The disorder generally prevailed and the control of Zabulistan changed hands frequently. Taking advantage of the situation, the Shahis stepped up activities on their western frontier. The result was the emergence of a small Hindu power at Ghazni, supported by the Shahis. "The authorities either themselves of early date or enshrining early information mention [Lawik](/wiki/Lawik "Lawik")", a Hindu, as the ruler at Ghazni, before this place was taken over by the Turkish slave governor of the Samanids.[[138]](#cite_note-138)
* **Jayapala:** With [Jayapala](/wiki/Jayapala "Jayapala"), a new dynasty started ruling over the former Shahi kingdom of southeastern Afghanistan and the change over was smooth and consensual. On his coronation, Jayapala used the additional name-suffix *Deva* from his predecessor's dynasty in addition to the *pala* name-ending of his own family. (With Kabul lost during the lifetime of Jayapaladeva, his successors – Anandapala, Trilochanapala and Bhimapala – reverted to their own family *pala*-ending names.) Jayapala did not issue any coins in his own name. Bull and Horseman coins with the legend Samantadeva, in billon, seem to have been struck during Jayapala's reign. As the successor of Bhima, Jayapala was a Shahi monarch of the state of Kabul, which now included Punjab. Minhaj-ud-din describes Jayapala as "the greatest of the Rais of Hindustan."[[132]](#cite_note-Tabaqat-i-Nasiri'_p._82-132)

### Balkh

From historical evidence, it appears [Tokharistan](/wiki/Tokharistan "Tokharistan") (Bactria) was the only area heavily colonized by Arabs where [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") flourished and the only area incorporated into the Arab empire where Sanskrit studies were pursued up to the conquest.[[139]](#cite_note-139) [Hui'Chao](/wiki/Hyecho "Hyecho"), who visited around 726, mentions that the Arabs ruled it and all the inhabitants were [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhists "Buddhists").[[140]](#cite_note-140) [Balkh](/wiki/Balkh "Balkh")'s final conquest was undertaken by [Qutayba ibn Muslim](/wiki/Qutayba_ibn_Muslim "Qutayba ibn Muslim") in 705.[[141]](#cite_note-141) Among Balkh's Buddhist monasteries, the largest was *[Nava Vihara](/wiki/Nava_Vihara "Nava Vihara")*, later Persianized to *Naw Bahara* after the Islamic conquest of Balkh.[[142]](#cite_note-142) It is not known how long it continued to serve as a place of worship after the conquest. Accounts of early Arabs offer contradictory narratives.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

### Ghur

[Amir Suri](/wiki/Amir_Suri "Amir Suri"), a king of the [Ghurid dynasty](/wiki/Ghurid_dynasty "Ghurid dynasty"), in the [Ghor](/wiki/Ghor "Ghor") region of present-day central Afghanistan, and his son [Muhammad ibn Suri](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Suri "Muhammad ibn Suri"), despite bearing Arabic names were Buddhists.[[144]](#cite_note-144) During their rule from the 9th century to the 10th century, they were considered [pagans](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") by the surrounding Muslim people, and it was only during the reign of Muhammad's son [Abu Ali ibn Muhammad](/wiki/Abu_Ali_ibn_Muhammad "Abu Ali ibn Muhammad") that the Ghurid dynasty became an Islamic dynasty. Amir Suri was a descendant of the Ghurid king [Amir Banji](/w/index.php?title=Amir_Banji&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amir Banji (page does not exist)"), whose rule was legitimized by the [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid") [caliph](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph") [Harun al-Rashid](/wiki/Harun_al-Rashid "Harun al-Rashid"). He is known to have fought the [Saffarid](/wiki/Saffarid "Saffarid") ruler [Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar](/wiki/Ya%27qub_ibn_al-Layth_al-Saffar "Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar"), who managed to conquer much of [Khurasan](/wiki/Khurasan "Khurasan") except [Ghur](/wiki/Ghur "Ghur").[[145]](#cite_note-145) Ghur remained a pagan enclave until the 11th century. [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni"), who raided it, left Muslim precepts to teach Islam to the local population. The region became Muslim by 12th century though the historian [Satish Chandra](/wiki/Satish_Chandra_(historian) "Satish Chandra (historian)") states that [Mahayana Buddhism](/wiki/Mahayana_Buddhism "Mahayana Buddhism") is believed to have existed until the end of the century.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

### Nuristan

The vast area extending from modern [Nuristan](/wiki/Nuristan "Nuristan") to Kashmir (styled "Peristan" by A. M. Cacopardo) containing host of "[Kafir](/wiki/Nuristanis "Nuristanis")" cultures and Indo-European languages that became Islamized over a long period. Earlier, it was surrounded by Buddhist areas. The Islamization of the nearby [Badakhshan](/wiki/Badakhshan "Badakhshan") began in the 8th century and Peristan was completely surrounded by Muslim states in the 16th century with Islamization of [Baltistan](/wiki/Baltistan "Baltistan"). The Buddhist states temporarily brought literacy and state rule into the region. The decline of Buddhism resulted in it becoming heavily isolated.[[147]](#cite_note-academia-147)

Successive wave of [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") immigration, before or during 16th and 17th centuries, displaced the original Kafirs and [Pashayi people](/wiki/Pashayi_people "Pashayi people") from [Kunar Valley](/wiki/Kunar_Valley "Kunar Valley") and Laghman valley, the two eastern provinces near [Jalalabad](/wiki/Jalalabad "Jalalabad"), to the less fertile mountains.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Before their conversion, the *Kafir* people of [Kafiristan](/wiki/Kafiristan "Kafiristan") practiced a form of [ancient Hinduism](/wiki/Ancient_Hinduism "Ancient Hinduism") infused with locally developed accretions.[[149]](#cite_note-149) The region from [Nuristan](/wiki/Nuristan "Nuristan") to [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") (styled *Peristan* by A. M. Cacopardo) was host to a vast number of "Kafir" cultures.[[147]](#cite_note-academia-147) They were called Kafirs due to their enduring paganism, remaining politically independent until being conquered and forcibly converted by Afghan Amir [Abdul Rahman Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Rahman_Khan "Abdul Rahman Khan") in 1895–1896[[150]](#cite_note-150) while others also converted to avoid paying *[jizya](/wiki/Jizya "Jizya")*.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

In 1020–21, Sultan Mahmud of Ghazna led a campaign against Kafiristan and the people of the "pleasant valleys of Nur and Qirat" according to Gardizi.[[152]](#cite_note-152) These people worshipped the lion.[[153]](#cite_note-153) [Mohammad Habib](/wiki/Mohammad_Habib "Mohammad Habib") however considers they might have been worshipping Buddha in form of a lion (*Sakya Sinha*).[[154]](#cite_note-154) [Ramesh Chandra Majumdar](/wiki/Ramesh_Chandra_Majumdar "Ramesh Chandra Majumdar") states they had a Hindu temple which was destroyed by Mahmud's general.[[155]](#cite_note-155)

One key source of this knowledge is a warm tribute paid to [Sylvain Lévi](/wiki/Sylvain_L%C3%A9vi "Sylvain Lévi") and his ideas of an expansive, civilising India by Jawaharlal Nehru himself, in his celebrated book, *The Discovery of India*, which was written during one of Nehru's periods of imprisonment by the British authorities, first published in 1946, and reprinted many times since.... The ideas of both Lévi and the Greater India scholars were known to Nehru through his close intellectual links with Tagore. Thus Lévi's notion of ancient Indian voyagers leaving their invisible 'imprints' throughout east and southeast Asia was for Nehru a recapitulation of Tagore's vision of nationhood, that is an idealisation of India as a benign and uncoercive world civiliser and font of global enlightenment. This was clearly a perspective which defined the Greater India phenomenon as a process of religious and spiritual tutelage, but it was not a Hindu supremacist idea of India's mission to the lands of the Trans-Gangetic *Sarvabhumi* or *Bharat Varsha*."
40. **[^](#cite_ref-40)** [Narasimhaiah (1986)](#CITEREFNarasimhaiah1986) Quote: "To him (Nehru), the so-called practical approach meant, in practice, shameless expediency, and so he would say, "the sooner we are not practical, the better". He rebuked a Member of Indian Parliament who sought to revive the concept of *Greater India* by saying that 'the honorable Member lived in the days of Bismarck; Bismarck is dead, and his politics more dead!' He would consistently plead for an idealistic approach and such power as the language wields is the creation of idealism—politics' arch enemy—which, however, liberates the leader of a national movement from narrow nationalism, thus igniting in the process a dead fact of history, in the sneer, "For him the Bastille has not fallen!" Though Nehru was not to the language born, his utterances show a remarkable capacity for introspection and sense of moral responsibility in commenting on political processes."
41. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** [Wheatley (1982](#CITEREFWheatley1982), pp. 27–28) Quote: "The tide of revisionism that is currently sweeping through Southeast Asian historiography has in effect taken us back almost to the point where we have to consider reevaluating almost every text bearing on the protohistoric period and many from later times. Although this may seem a daunting proposition, it is nonetheless supremely worth attempting, for the process by which the peoples of western Southeast Asia came to think of themselves as part of *Bharatavarsa* (even though they had no conception of "India" as we know it) represents one of the most impressive instances of large-scale acculturation in the history of the world. [Sylvain Levi](/wiki/Sylvain_Levi "Sylvain Levi") was perhaps overenthusiastic when he claimed that India produced her definitive masterpieces – he was thinking of Angkor and the Borobudur – through the efforts of foreigners or on foreign soil. Those masterpieces were not strictly Indian achievements: rather were they the outcome of a Eutychian fusion of natures so melded together as to constitute a single cultural process in which Southeast Asia was the matrix and South Asia the mediatrix."
